articles.title: The Supreme Court of Canada
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog published an installment of its recurring series comparing supreme courts around the world, this time focusing on the Supreme Court of Canada. The piece interviews experts on how the Canadian high court operates and how it compares to the U.S. Supreme Court.
articles.author: Zachary Shemtob

articles.title: The biggest names on the briefs
articles.summary: Adam Feldman's Empirical SCOTUS series examines data on which advocates appear most frequently on Supreme Court briefs. The analysis looks at patterns in briefing to shed light on the justices' decision-making processes.
articles.author: Adam Feldman

articles.title: The remaining questions after the Supreme Court’s tariffs ruling
articles.summary: Following the Supreme Court's recent ruling on President Trump's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs, SCOTUSblog analyzes the unresolved legal questions that remain. The decision addressed the 1977 law's grant of presidential power to regulate commerce during foreign-created national emergencies, but several issues about its scope and application are still open.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Tuesday, March 17
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily roundup for March 17, 2026, marks St. Patrick's Day by highlighting Supreme Court justices of Irish descent. The post serves as a brief daily digest of court-related news and trivia.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Chief Justice Says Personal Attacks on Judges Are ‘Dangerous’ and Must Stop
articles.summary: Chief Justice John Roberts publicly stated that personal attacks on judges are 'dangerous' and must stop, marking his first public remarks since President Trump harshly criticized the justices who ruled against his tariff policies. Roberts's comments signal concern about threats to judicial independence and the rule of law.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: Justices will hear argument on Trump administration’s removal of protected status for Syrian and Haitian nationals
articles.summary: The Supreme Court announced it will hear oral arguments on whether the Trump administration can terminate Temporary Protected Status (TPS) for several thousand Syrian nationals and approximately 350,000 Haitian nationals. This case is part of the administration's broader mass deportation efforts and could have significant implications for protected status programs.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Haitian nationals ask court to deny Trump administration’s request to remove their protected status
articles.summary: A group of Haitian nationals filed a brief urging the Supreme Court to leave intact a federal judge's ruling in Washington, D.C., that allows them to remain in the United States under Temporary Protected Status. They are opposing the Trump administration's request to strip their protected status as part of its immigration enforcement agenda.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: a response to Pete Patterson
articles.summary: Constitutional law scholars Akhil and Vikram Amar published a response to Pete Patterson regarding the issue of birthright citizenship, as part of their recurring "Brothers in Law" series. The piece examines constitutional text and Supreme Court precedent on the question of who is entitled to citizenship at birth under the 14th Amendment.
articles.author: Akhil and Vikram Amar

articles.title: A 95th birthday tribute to legendary SCOTUSblog reporter Lyle Denniston
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog published a tribute to legendary Supreme Court reporter Lyle Denniston on his 95th birthday. Denniston, who served as SCOTUSblog's primary reporter from 2004 to 2016, began his journalism career in 1948 and is renowned for his decades of coverage of the Court.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: SCOTUStoday: Trump v. the Fed
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily recap noted the sixth anniversary of the Supreme Court postponing its March argument session due to the COVID-19 pandemic, while also discussing current tensions between the Trump administration and the Federal Reserve. The piece draws parallels between past and present disruptions to the Court's operations and broader governmental conflicts.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: Supreme Court Defers Decision on Trump’s Bid to End Protections for Migrants
articles.summary: The Supreme Court deferred an immediate decision on the Trump administration's bid to end Temporary Protected Status for migrants from Haiti and Syria, opting instead to schedule oral arguments on the matter. The case is central to the president's mass deportation efforts and could affect hundreds of thousands of migrants currently living in the United States.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: Supreme Court to consider whether Trump can end protected status for Syrians, Haitians
articles.summary: The Supreme Court agreed to consider whether the Trump administration can end Temporary Protected Status for Syrian and Haitian nationals, a decision that could have far-reaching consequences for other legal challenges to the government's efforts to terminate TPS for hundreds of thousands of migrants. The case represents a major test of executive authority over immigration protections.
articles.author: Julian Mark

articles.title: Announcement of opinions for Friday, March 20
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog announced that on Friday, March 20, the Supreme Court may release opinions in one or more argued cases from the current term. The blog plans to provide live coverage as any decisions are handed down.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: Does legislative history have a judicial future?
articles.summary: This analytical piece examines whether legislative history still has a role in judicial interpretation, particularly in the context of the Supreme Court's approach to administrative law and agency power. It is part of a recurring series on major questions in administrative law.
articles.author: Adam White

articles.title: Is Justice Alito jumping the gun on voting rights?
articles.summary: This opinion piece questions whether Justice Alito is prematurely addressing voting rights issues in his Supreme Court opinions. The article is part of a recurring series analyzing the effects of the Court's rulings and procedures on law and other institutions.
articles.author: Carolyn Shapiro

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Friday, March 13
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily roundup for March 13 notes the historical anniversary of President Chester A. Arthur's nomination of Justice Samuel Blatchford to the Supreme Court on this date in 1882. Blatchford, described as a precocious talent, attended what is now Columbia University.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: Originalism 101
articles.summary: The article critiques the originalist arguments being made in Trump v. Barbara, the birthright citizenship case before the Supreme Court. It suggests that while many parties claim to use originalism as their interpretive method, the Trump administration's arguments fall short of sound originalist analysis regarding the 14th Amendment's citizenship clause.
articles.author: Samarth Desai

articles.title: An interview with Jerry Goldman, founder of the Oyez Project
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog interviews Jerry Goldman, the founder of the Oyez Project, as part of a new series on people who have shaped public understanding of the Supreme Court. The conversation covers Goldman's pioneering work in making Supreme Court oral arguments and information accessible to the public online.
articles.author: Nora Collins

articles.title: When presidents attack the Supreme Court
articles.summary: The article examines the historical and contemporary phenomenon of presidents publicly attacking the Supreme Court, focusing on President Trump's recent criticisms following a ruling against his administration. It places Trump's remarks in the broader context of past presidential conflicts with the Court.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Thursday, March 12
articles.summary: This daily Supreme Court recap notes the historical event on March 12, 1804, when the House of Representatives voted to impeach Justice Samuel Chase for alleged abuses of power, including refusing to dismiss biased jurors. The piece serves as a brief historical snapshot tied to the current Court's calendar.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: Supreme Court Justices Spar Publicly
articles.summary: Supreme Court justices have clashed publicly over the Court's handling of cases on the emergency or "shadow" docket. The dispute highlights ongoing tensions among the justices about the propriety and frequency of emergency rulings without full briefing and argument.
articles.author: Sarah Isgur, David French

articles.title: Trump administration urges Supreme Court to allow it to revoke protected status for Haitian nationals
articles.summary: The Trump administration asked the Supreme Court to stay a federal judge's ruling that blocked the government from ending Temporary Protected Status (TPS) for Haitian nationals. The administration is seeking emergency relief to allow it to revoke the protected status while litigation continues.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: The 14th Amendment’s citizenship clause does not codify English principles of subjectship
articles.summary: A SCOTUSblog analysis argues that the 14th Amendment's citizenship clause does not simply adopt English common-law principles of subjectship. The piece engages with the legal debate surrounding President Trump's executive order attempting to restrict birthright citizenship for children born to undocumented immigrants.
articles.author: Pete Patterson

articles.title: The First Amendment’s application to public university students: an explainer
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog published an explainer on how the First Amendment applies to students at public universities. The article surveys the legal landscape around campus free speech, a topic that has gained renewed attention due to protests related to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict at various universities.
articles.author: Alex Rivenbark

articles.title: Abandoning the separation of powers in times of war
articles.summary: In a column by Erwin Chemerinsky, the piece examines how the Supreme Court has historically weakened separation-of-powers principles during wartime. The analysis considers the broader implications of the Court's decisions for civil liberties and executive power when the nation is engaged in armed conflict.
articles.author: Erwin Chemerinsky

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Wednesday, March 11
articles.summary: The daily SCOTUStoday roundup for March 11 covers a range of Supreme Court-related news, including a report on lawsuits over AI bots allegedly practicing law without a license. The digest compiles the day's notable stories related to the Court and the legal profession.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Trump Administration Asks Supreme Court to End Protections for Haitian Immigrants
articles.summary: The New York Times reports that the Trump administration has asked the Supreme Court to end Temporary Protected Status deportation protections for Haitian immigrants. The administration has also sought to terminate similar protections for other immigrant groups, including Syrians.
articles.author: Abbie VanSickle and Miriam Jordan

articles.title: The how and why of gun control
articles.summary: The article discusses the Supreme Court's oral argument in United States v. Hemani, a case related to gun control and the Second Amendment. It is part of a recurring series analyzing Second Amendment constitutional litigation and examines the legal questions at stake in the case.
articles.author: Haley Proctor

articles.title: SCOTUSblog’s new podcast partners
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog announced the addition of two new podcasts, Amarica's Constitution and Divided Argument, to its podcast lineup alongside Advisory Opinions. The new podcast partners will maintain editorial independence while being featured on the SCOTUSblog platform.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: legal takeaways of mice and men and elephants and dogs
articles.summary: Legal scholars Akhil and Vikram Amar analyze the legal takeaways from the Supreme Court's handling of birthright citizenship cases. The article examines how the Court's rulings compare to the text of the Constitution itself, as part of a recurring constitutional analysis series.
articles.author: Akhil and Vikram Amar

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Tuesday, March 10
articles.summary: The SCOTUStoday daily roundup announces the addition of new podcasts to SCOTUSblog's lineup and highlights a joint episode featuring hosts from multiple Supreme Court–focused podcasts. The episode covers a range of current Supreme Court developments.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Two Supreme Court Justices Debate Handling of Trump Emergency Cases
articles.summary: Justices Ketanji Brown Jackson and Brett Kavanaugh publicly debated how the Supreme Court should handle emergency requests, particularly those related to Trump administration cases. The rare joint appearance revealed sharp disagreements over the Court's use of its emergency or "shadow" docket.
articles.author: Abbie VanSickle

articles.title: Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson criticizes Supreme Court emergency rulings
articles.summary: Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson criticized the Supreme Court's handling of emergency rulings during a public forum alongside Justice Brett Kavanaugh. The two justices offered contrasting perspectives on President Trump's wins before the high court through emergency proceedings.
articles.author: Justin Jouvenal

articles.title: Court agrees to hear case on environmental laws, does not act on several Second Amendment challenges
articles.summary: The Supreme Court added one new case to its docket involving a technical dispute over the interaction between two federal environmental laws. The court did not take action on several pending Second Amendment challenges, leaving those cases unresolved for now.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: In birthright citizenship case, Justice Department urges court to treat an old concept in a new way
articles.summary: In the ongoing birthright citizenship litigation, the Justice Department is urging the Supreme Court to reinterpret a longstanding constitutional concept in a novel way as part of President Trump's immigration agenda. The article analyzes the government's legal arguments about the scope of birthright citizenship under the 14th Amendment.
articles.author: César Cuauhtémoc García Hernández

articles.title: The dissent that believed the Olympics belong to everyone
articles.summary: This article examines a historic Supreme Court dissent related to the Olympics and intellectual property or trademark rights, arguing that the Olympic brand should be more publicly accessible. The piece is part of a recurring series on influential Supreme Court dissents that have shaped American law and culture.
articles.author: Anastasia Boden

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Monday, March 9
articles.summary: A daily Supreme Court news roundup highlights a new NBC News poll showing that only 22% of registered U.S. voters have significant confidence in the Supreme Court. The low confidence numbers reflect ongoing public skepticism about the institution.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Supreme Court to hear dispute over disposal of Air Force munitions on Guam
articles.summary: The Supreme Court agreed to hear a case involving a dispute between environmentalists in Guam and the Trump administration over the Air Force's disposal of hazardous munitions on a beach. The case centers on the interaction between federal environmental laws governing the cleanup of military waste.
articles.author: Justin Jouvenal

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: the exceptions provide the rule
articles.summary: The article examines the debate over birthright citizenship under the 14th Amendment, focusing on how the exceptions to the clause "subject to the jurisdiction" shape the interpretation of who qualifies for citizenship at birth. It analyzes the constitutional text and its historical context in the ongoing legal and political battles over birthright citizenship.
articles.author: Samarth Desai

articles.title: The emergency docket’s critics have it backwards
articles.summary: Legal scholar Stephanie Barclay argues that critics of the Supreme Court's emergency or "shadow" docket have the analysis backwards. The piece defends the Court's use of emergency orders, suggesting the practice serves important constitutional and practical functions rather than undermining judicial norms.
articles.author: Stephanie Barclay

articles.title: Will the mystery of the Dobbs leak ever be solved?
articles.summary: The article revisits the still-unsolved leak of the Supreme Court's draft opinion in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, prompted by renewed attention to court security after Justice Clarence Thomas shifted a conference appearance to virtual due to security concerns. It discusses the ongoing mystery and implications for the Court's internal operations.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Friday, March 6
articles.summary: This daily Supreme Court roundup notes the historical anniversary of the Court's 1857 decision in Dred Scott v. Sandford, which held that an enslaved man who had lived in free territory was not a citizen and could not sue in federal court. The post provides a brief recap of Court-related news for March 6.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Justices poised to adopt exceptions to federal criminal defendants’ appellate waivers
articles.summary: The Supreme Court heard oral argument in Hunter v. United States, which concerns what exceptions should apply when federal criminal defendants waive their right to appeal as part of plea agreements. Based on the argument, the justices appeared inclined to recognize certain exceptions to these appellate waivers.
articles.author: Richard Cooke

articles.title: Syrian nationals urge Supreme Court to keep ruling in place allowing them to stay in the United States
articles.summary: A group of Syrian nationals asked the Supreme Court to preserve a federal judge's ruling that allows them to remain in the United States. The case involves immigration protections and the government's ability to remove the individuals.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Court grapples with whether federal law supersedes negligent hiring claims against freight brokers
articles.summary: The Supreme Court heard oral arguments in Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC, debating whether federal law preempts state negligent hiring claims against freight brokers. The case could determine whether brokers can be held liable under state law when accidents occur involving carriers they hired.
articles.author: Nora Collins

articles.title: Supreme Court rules that New Jersey Transit can be sued in other states
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled in Galette v. New Jersey Transit Corporation that New Jersey Transit can be sued in courts outside New Jersey. The decision allows two men seriously injured by NJ Transit buses in New York and Pennsylvania to pursue their claims in those states.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Thursday, March 5
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily roundup covers several developments, including the Trump administration's timeline for resolving tariff refund disputes and other ongoing Supreme Court activity. The summary touches on multiple cases and procedural updates from the Court's Wednesday session.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: An Assertive Supreme Court Turns to Curbing State Courts
articles.summary: The Supreme Court issued orders this week that liberal justices criticized as an expansion of the emergency docket, this time to curb state court actions. The article describes an increasingly assertive Court willing to intervene in state court proceedings through its shadow docket.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: Opinions for Wednesday, March 4
articles.summary: The Supreme Court released opinions in two cases on Wednesday, March 4: Urias-Orellana v. Bondi and Galette v. New Jersey Transit Corp. SCOTUSblog provided live coverage as the decisions were handed down.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: an empirical analysis of supposedly originalist briefs
articles.summary: Legal scholars Akhil and Vikram Amar analyze amicus briefs filed in a birthright citizenship case before the Supreme Court, evaluating whether the briefs' originalist arguments hold up under scrutiny. The piece is part of their recurring "Brothers in Law" series comparing the Court's reasoning with constitutional text.
articles.author: Akhil and Vikram Amar

articles.title: The SCOTUS attorney switcheroo
articles.summary: Adam Feldman's "Empirical SCOTUS" series examines the phenomenon of attorney switches at the Supreme Court, analyzing data from oral arguments and opinions to shed light on how changes in legal representation may affect case outcomes. The piece uses empirical data to explore patterns in Supreme Court advocacy.
articles.author: Adam Feldman

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Wednesday, March 4
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily briefing previewed the Supreme Court's fourth opinion day in less than two weeks, with live blogging coverage beginning at 9:30 a.m. EST on Wednesday, March 4. The post set the stage for the day's expected opinion releases.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Court unanimously sides with government in immigration dispute
articles.summary: The Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of the federal government in Urias-Orellana v. Bondi, an immigration case. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson wrote the opinion holding that federal courts of appeals must follow certain procedural requirements when reviewing immigration decisions.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: Déjà vu all over again
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's Relist Watch column examines cert petitions that the Supreme Court has relisted for its upcoming conference. The column notes the Court is continuing to consider various petitions that have been held over for further discussion.
articles.author: John Elwood

articles.title: The UK Supreme Court
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog launched a new recurring series comparing supreme courts around the world, starting with the UK Supreme Court. The article interviews experts on how the UK's highest court compares to the U.S. Supreme Court in structure and function.
articles.author: Zachary Shemtob

articles.title: The justices’ troubling message to lower courts
articles.summary: In a column on criminal justice and civil rights, the author discusses how the Supreme Court recently issued summary reversals that send a troubling message to lower courts. The piece examines the implications of these decisions for how lower courts should handle similar cases going forward.
articles.author: Daniel Harawa

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Tuesday, March 3
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily newsletter rounds up the latest Supreme Court news and legal headlines for March 3. The newsletter references recent notable legal news items and Court developments.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: Court sides with parents in dispute over California policies on transgender students
articles.summary: The Supreme Court granted a request from California parents to reinstate a federal district court ruling that blocks California schools from hiding students' transgender status from their parents. The decision sides with parents and religious educators who challenged state policies on transgender student notifications.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Supreme Court grants Republicans’ request to pause order to redraw New York congressional map
articles.summary: The Supreme Court granted Republicans' emergency request to pause a lower court order requiring New York to redraw its congressional map ahead of the 2026 elections. The ruling allows New York to proceed with its existing congressional districts despite concerns that the map illegally diluted minority voters' power.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Supreme Court Sides With Religious Parents, Blocking California’s Trans Student Policy
articles.summary: The Supreme Court sided with Christian teachers and parents who challenged California policies they say require schools to conceal students' transgender status from their parents. The ruling blocks the state's trans student notification policies from being enforced.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: In Republican Win, Supreme Court Retains G.O.P. District in New York
articles.summary: In an emergency ruling, the Supreme Court preserved the congressional district of a Republican congresswoman in New York, despite a lower court finding that it illegally diluted minority voting power. The decision is a significant win for Republicans heading into the 2026 election cycle.
articles.author: Abbie VanSickle and Grace Ashford

articles.title: Supreme Court sides with Christian parents in battle over school trans policies
articles.summary: The Supreme Court sided with Christian parents challenging a California law that prohibits school policies requiring employees to report students' sexual orientation and gender identity to parents. The ruling reinstates a lower court order blocking the state's transgender student notification restrictions.
articles.author: Julian Mark

articles.title: Supreme Court blocks redrawing of Republican’s district in New York
articles.summary: The Supreme Court blocked the redrawing of a Republican-held congressional district in New York that a state trial court had ordered redrawn to include more Black and Latino voters. The decision preserves the current district lines for upcoming elections.
articles.author: Julian Mark, Justin Jouvenal

articles.title: Court turns down several cases, including on filing fees for indigent prisoners and ability of felons to possess guns
articles.summary: The Supreme Court declined to hear several cases on Monday, including one about filing fees for indigent prisoners and another about felons' gun possession rights. The court's three Democratic-appointed justices dissented from the denial of the prisoner filing-fee case.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Birthright citizenship: A note on foundlings and comments on four complementary amicus briefs
articles.summary: This article discusses the legal and historical concept of foundlings (babies of unknown parentage) in the context of the birthright citizenship cases before the Supreme Court. It comments on four amicus briefs that complement the arguments in the litigation challenging executive action on birthright citizenship.
articles.author: Akhil and Vikram Amar

articles.title: Supreme Court skeptical of law banning drug users from possessing firearms
articles.summary: The Supreme Court appeared skeptical during oral arguments of a federal law that prohibits drug users from possessing firearms. The case involves a Texas man indicted under the statute, and a majority of justices questioned whether the law is constitutionally permissible under the Second Amendment.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Oral argument live blog for Monday, March 2
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog hosted a live blog covering Monday's oral argument in United States v. Hemani, which concerns whether a federal statute prohibiting gun possession by users of illegal drugs is constitutional. The live blog provided real-time updates as the justices questioned the attorneys.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: Justices to consider breadth of a federal defendant’s waiver of appeal
articles.summary: The Supreme Court will hear arguments in Hunter v. United States on Tuesday, March 3, addressing how broadly a federal defendant's waiver of the right to appeal should be interpreted. The case examines the scope of appeal waivers that defendants agree to as part of plea deals.
articles.author: Richard Cooke

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Monday, March 2
articles.summary: This daily summary from SCOTUSblog highlights the day's key Supreme Court activity, centered on the oral argument in United States v. Hemani about the federal ban on gun possession by drug users. It also points readers to an animated explainer and a live blog covering the argument.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Supreme Court Appears Skeptical of Law Banning Drug Users From Owning Guns
articles.summary: The Supreme Court appeared skeptical of a federal law banning drug users from owning firearms, with a majority of justices voicing concerns that the law is overbroad. Justices questioned whether the statute improperly lumps occasional drug users with addicts who pose genuine public safety threats.
articles.author: Abbie VanSickle

articles.title: Could a Supreme Court Case Help Hunter Biden Get His Law License Back?
articles.summary: The article discusses how the Supreme Court's consideration of the federal gun law used to convict Hunter Biden could affect his efforts to regain his law license. Biden says he is closely watching the Hemani case, which tests the constitutionality of the statute that prohibits drug users from possessing firearms.
articles.author: Abbie VanSickle

articles.title: Supreme Court weighs whether smoking weed can eliminate your right to a gun
articles.summary: The Supreme Court heard arguments on whether a federal law barring marijuana users from possessing guns violates the Second Amendment. The central legal question is whether the restriction is consistent with the nation's historical tradition of firearm regulation, as required by the Court's recent precedents.
articles.author: Julian Mark

articles.title: Could the Supreme Court’s Voting Rights Act Decision Affect the Midterms?
articles.summary: The Supreme Court is expected to issue a major ruling under the Voting Rights Act that could significantly reshape congressional district maps across the country. The timing of the decision is critical, as it could impact redistricting efforts ahead of the upcoming midterm elections. The article examines how different outcomes and timing scenarios could affect the political landscape.
articles.author: Nick Corasaniti, Ashley Wu and Elena Shao

articles.title: Announcement of opinions for Wednesday, March 4
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog announced it will live-blog the potential release of opinions in argued cases on Wednesday, March 4. The court may hand down decisions in one or more cases from the current term.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: Supreme Court to consider whether freight brokers can be held liable for negligent hiring
articles.summary: The Supreme Court will hear oral arguments in Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II on March 4, addressing whether a federal law originally designed to address state trucking regulations preempts state-law claims holding freight brokers liable for negligent hiring. The case concerns the scope of federal preemption in the trucking industry.
articles.author: Nora Collins

articles.title: Justices appear dubious of challenge to constitutionality of foreclosure sales
articles.summary: During oral arguments in Pung v. Isabella County, the justices appeared skeptical of a constitutional challenge to foreclosure sales. While the justices seemed confident they should hear the case, they expressed doubts about the merits of the challenge to the constitutionality of the foreclosure process at issue.
articles.author: Ronald Mann

articles.title: The major debate over major questions in the tariffs decision is only the beginning
articles.summary: A SCOTUSblog analysis examines the Supreme Court's recent decision striking down the president's tariffs and the role the major questions doctrine played in the ruling. The article argues that the debate over the major questions doctrine in the tariffs case is just the beginning of broader legal battles over executive power and regulatory authority.
articles.author: Abbe R. Gluck

articles.title: United States v. Hemani: an animated explainer
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog released an animated explainer video about United States v. Hemani, one of the important upcoming cases of the 2025-26 term. The video is the first in a planned series done in partnership with Briefly to make key Supreme Court cases more accessible to the public.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Friday, February 27
articles.summary: The daily SCOTUSblog roundup for February 27 highlights the launch of a new animated video series covering important cases of the current Supreme Court term, beginning with a case explainer. The post provides a summary of the day's Supreme Court news and developments.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas and Nora Collins

articles.title: Justice Thomas Bemoans Incivility as Security Prompts Cancellation of In-Person Speech
articles.summary: Justice Clarence Thomas lamented growing incivility and security threats facing members of the judiciary after an in-person speaking engagement was canceled due to security concerns. He instead participated remotely in a closed-door session of a legal conference, underscoring the heightened threats facing jurists in recent years.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: Court rejects ICE contractor’s right to immediate appeal
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled in GEO Group v. Menocal that a private contractor for ICE cannot pursue an immediate appeal from a district court judgment. The decision limits the ability of government contractors to use interlocutory appeals in such cases.
articles.author: Ronald Mann

articles.title: Beach blasts and unusually dangerous weapons
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's Relist Watch column examines cert petitions that have been relisted for an upcoming Supreme Court conference, focusing on cases involving beach blasts and unusually dangerous weapons. The column tracks petitions the justices are giving additional consideration before deciding whether to grant review.
articles.author: John Elwood

articles.title: Trump administration asks justices to allow it to remove protected status from Syrian nationals
articles.summary: The Trump administration asked the Supreme Court to stay a federal judge's ruling that blocked the termination of Temporary Protected Status for Syrian nationals. The administration seeks to end the program as part of broader immigration enforcement efforts.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Court to hear argument on whether and when drug users may possess firearms
articles.summary: The Supreme Court will hear oral arguments in United States v. Hemani, a case examining whether and when drug users may be prohibited from possessing firearms. This is the second gun-rights case of the 2025-26 term, following an earlier case involving Hawaii gun regulations.
articles.author: Amy Howe

articles.title: Court rules criminal defendants may be prohibited from discussing ongoing testimony with counsel during an overnight recess
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that trial courts may order criminal defendants not to discuss their ongoing testimony with their lawyers during overnight recesses. The decision addresses the balance between a defendant's right to counsel and the integrity of trial testimony.
articles.author: Evan Lee

articles.title: How can the Supreme Court protect electoral integrity?
articles.summary: An opinion column by Edward B. Foley explores how the Supreme Court can protect electoral integrity, as part of a recurring series on election law and democracy. The piece discusses the Court's confrontation with election-related cases and its role in safeguarding democratic processes.
articles.author: Edward Foley

articles.title: SCOTUStoday for Thursday, February 26
articles.summary: SCOTUSblog's daily roundup for February 26 highlights a new poll showing 57% of Americans approve of a recent tariffs ruling by the Supreme Court. The post also includes a morning reads section with additional coverage of Court-related news.
articles.author: Kelsey Dallas

articles.title: Trump Administration Asks Supreme Court to End Protections for Syrian Migrants
articles.summary: The Trump administration asked the Supreme Court to end Temporary Protected Status for Syrian migrants, part of the president's broader mass deportation efforts. The request seeks to overturn a lower court ruling that blocked the termination of the program.
articles.author: Ann E. Marimow

articles.title: Justice Gorsuch’s Tariffs Warning
articles.summary: Justice Gorsuch issued a warning in a tariffs case about the dangers of granting new powers to the president, noting that such powers are easy to grant but nearly impossible to reclaim. The commentary highlights concerns about the expansion of executive authority through trade policy.
articles.author: Adam Liptak

articles.title: Justices send litigation about tainted baby food back to state court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court decided Hain Celestial Group v. Palmquist, sending litigation over tainted baby food back to state court. The ruling addresses what happens when a federal district court incorrectly rules on its own jurisdiction in a case that was removed from state court.
articles.author: Ronald Mann

articles.title: Justices reveal little about whether the deadline for removing cases to federal court can be excused
articles.summary: The Supreme Court heard arguments but revealed little about whether the 30-day deadline for removing cases from state to federal court can be excused. The case addresses an important procedural question about the flexibility of removal deadlines under federal law.
articles.author: Bradley Joondeph

articles.title: Opinions for Wednesday, February 25
articles.summary: The Supreme Court released opinions in two cases on February 25: Villarreal v. Texas and The GEO Group, Inc. v. Menocal. SCOTUSblog provided live coverage as the decisions were handed down.
articles.author: SCOTUSblog

articles.title: John Roberts Is Losing Patience With Trump
articles.summary: An opinion piece analyzes Chief Justice John Roberts's opinion in a tariff case, arguing that a notable paragraph signals his growing frustration with the Trump administration. The article interprets Roberts's language as a deliberate rebuke of executive overreach.
articles.author: Linda Greenhouse

articles.title: Assessing the Supreme Court’s Tariff Ruling
articles.summary: Readers respond to the Supreme Court's ruling rejecting certain tariffs imposed by the executive branch. The letters discuss the implications of the decision for presidential trade authority and the separation of powers.
